title: Two Pointers — Dry Run & Recall
oneSentence: Trace existing problems (Two Sum II, Palindrome, 3Sum, Trapping Rain, LinkedList Cycle) step-by-step.

twoSumII.title: Two Sum II — Sorted Array
twoSumII.description: Given a 1-indexed sorted array, find two numbers that sum to a target.
twoSumII.story: Shortest + tallest kids. Too short? Pick next taller. Too tall? Pick next shorter.
code: public static int[] solve(int[] nums, int target) {

steps.desc: Init: left pointer at 0 (value 2), right pointer at 3 (value 15).
Check sum: nums[left] + nums[right] = 2 + 15 = 17.
Sum (17) > target (9). It is too tall. We must narrow our right wall. Decrement right.
right pointer decremented to index 2 (value 11).
Check sum: nums[left] + nums[right] = 2 + 11 = 13.
Sum (13) > target (9). Still too tall. Decrement right.
right pointer decremented to index 1 (value 7).
Check sum: nums[left] + nums[right] = 2 + 7 = 9.
Sum (9) matches target (9)! Return 1-indexed result: [1, 2].

quiz.q: Why can we safely increment left if sum < target in Two Sum II?
options: Because the array is sorted, incrementing left moves it to a larger value, which increases the sum.
Because the array is sorted, incrementing left decreases the sum.
It is a random choice and we could also decrement right.
quiz.explain: Since the array is sorted in ascending order, incrementing the left pointer moves it to a larger value, which increases the total sum closer to the target.

palindrome.title: Valid Palindrome
palindrome.description: Check if a string is a palindrome, skipping non-alphanumeric chars and ignoring case.
palindrome.story: Two friends reading a banner from outside-in, skipping spaces & punctuation.

steps.desc: Init: left at index 0 ('a'), right at index 6 ('a').
Left character 'a' is valid alphanumeric. No skip needed.
Right character 'a' is valid alphanumeric. No skip needed.
Compare left ('a') & right ('a'). They match! Both step inward.
left increments to 1 (','), right decrements to 5 (' ').
Left skip: character at index 1 (',') and index 2 (' ') are not valid. left skips to index 3 ('b').
Right skip: character at index 5 (' ') and index 4 ('!') are not valid. right skips to index 3 ('b').
Now left (3) is not less than right (3). The loop terminates. Return true.

quiz.q: Why do we need left < right checks inside the inner character skip loops in Valid Palindrome?
options: To prevent pointers from moving out of bounds when skipping non-alphanumeric characters.
To speed up the execution to O(log N).
It has no functional purpose and is optional.
quiz.explain: If a string contains only non-alphanumeric characters (like ' ,,, '), the pointers would continue incrementing or decrementing past the string bounds without the left < right check.

threeSum.title: 3Sum
threeSum.description: Find all unique triplets in an array that sum to zero.
threeSum.story: For each person, find two others whose wealth exactly offsets yours to zero. Opposite-ends style.

steps.desc: Outer i=0 (val -4): Place left at 1 (val -1), right at 5 (val 2).
Sum = -4 + (-1) + 2 = -3 < 0. Too small! increment left to seek larger values.
left moves to index 2 (val -1). Sum is still -3 < 0. Increment left.
left moves to index 3 (val 0). Sum = -4 + 0 + 2 = -2 < 0. Increment left.
left moves to 4 (val 1). Sum = -4 + 1 + 2 = -1 < 0. Increment left.
left met right (5 == 5). Inner loop ends for i=0.
Outer i=1 (val -1): Place left at 2 (val -1), right at 5 (val 2).
Sum = -1 + (-1) + 2 = 0! MATCH found! Add [-1, -1, 2]. Increment left, decrement right.
left becomes 3 (val 0), right becomes 4 (val 1). No duplicates to skip.
Sum = -1 + 0 + 1 = 0! MATCH found! Add [-1, 0, 1]. Increment left, decrement right.
left crosses right (4 > 3). Inner loop ends. Return results.

quiz.q: In 3Sum, why is sorting the array initially a critical step?
options: It allows us to easily skip duplicate elements to avoid duplicate triplets and use the Two-Pointer approach.
It reduces the time complexity from O(N^2) to O(N log N).
It is required for Java's Arrays.asList to work.
quiz.explain: Sorting groups duplicates together. This allows us to skip duplicate values for the anchor i and pointers left/right, avoiding duplicate triplets.

trappingRain.title: Trapping Rain Water
trappingRain.description: Given wall heights, compute how much rainwater is trapped between them.
trappingRain.story: Bathtub walls. Always process the shorter wall first because it is the bottleneck.

steps.desc: Init: left=0 (height 0), right=3 (height 2). leftMax=0, rightMax=0, water=0.
Compare: height[left] (0) < height[right] (2). Left is the bottleneck!
height[left] (0) >= leftMax (0). Update leftMax to 0.
Move left to index 1.
Compare: height[left] (1) < height[right] (2). Left is still the bottleneck.
height[left] (1) > leftMax (0). Update leftMax to 1.
Move left to index 2.
Compare: height[left] (0) < height[right] (2). Left remains the bottleneck.
height[left] (0) < leftMax (1). Water trapped! water += 1 - 0 = 1 (Total: 1).
Move left to index 3.
Pointers met (left == right). Loop finishes. Return water (1).

quiz.q: What is the critical rule for moving pointers in Trapping Rain Water?
options: Always move the pointer that points to a smaller height first, because it limits the water level.
Always move both pointers inward at each step.
Always move the pointer pointing to the taller wall, to search for a new boundary.
quiz.explain: The shorter wall is always the bottleneck for holding water. We process the shorter side and compare it against its maximum boundary.

linkedListCycle.title: Linked List Cycle
linkedListCycle.description: Determine if a linked list contains a cycle using Floyd's Tortoise and Hare.
linkedListCycle.story: Tortoise (1 step) and Hare (2 steps) run on a circular track. Collide if there is a cycle.

steps.desc: Init: both slow pointer (slow) and fast pointer (fast) start at head node index 0 (val 3).
slow moves 1 step to index 1 (val 2). fast moves 2 steps to index 2 (val 0).
slow moves to index 2 (val 0). fast moves 2 steps: index 2 -> 3 (-4) -> cycles back to index 1 (val 2)!
slow moves to index 3 (-4). fast moves 2 steps: index 1 -> 2 -> 3 (-4). Collision at node -4! Return true.

quiz.q: In Floyd's Cycle Detection (Tortoise and Hare), why are the slow and fast pointers guaranteed to meet if there is a cycle?
options: The relative distance between them decreases by exactly 1 at each step once both are in the cycle.
The fast pointer moves backwards if it detects a cycle.
The slow pointer stops at the start of the cycle.
quiz.explain: Once both pointers are in the cycle, the fast pointer (speed 2) gains 1 step on the slow pointer (speed 1) at each iteration. Thus, the distance between them decreases by 1 until they meet.
